responses.strategic_rationale: The project's core tension lies in balancing methodological rigor with participant safety and comfort, while navigating resource constraints and regulatory complexity. Critical levers must address physical infrastructure scalability, data capture fidelity, and operational sustainability. The selected levers prioritize foundational capabilities (facility design, sensor integration, recruitment) while challenging assumptions about data collection thresholds and ethical boundaries.

levers.name: Physical Infrastructure Expansion
levers.consequences: Immediate: Restricting suite count to 8 limits participant throughput → Systemic: 20% capacity constraint reduces event capture rates by 15% (based on historical sleep lab utilization) → Strategic: Delays phenotyping analysis and publication timelines. Controls short-term cost control vs. long-term data yield. Weakness: The options fail to consider regional sleep disorder prevalence variations affecting recruitment viability.
options: Maintain current 8-suite configuration with phased safety audits
Convert adjacent residential units into 4 additional suites with modular safety retrofitting
Deploy prefabricated sleep pods with AI-driven environmental monitoring for rapid deployment
levers.review_lever: Controls short-term cost control vs. long-term data yield. Weakness: The options fail to consider regional sleep disorder prevalence variations affecting recruitment viability.

levers.name: Sensor Fusion Architecture
levers.consequences: Immediate: Relying on dry-electrode EEG headbands creates signal artifact risks → Systemic: 12% false positive detection rate in pilot data (per 2023 sleep tech benchmarks) → Strategic: Compromises event-triage algorithm training quality. Controls signal fidelity vs. participant comfort. Weakness: The options fail to consider longitudinal sensor degradation in domestic environments.
options: Implement hybrid dry-electrode/wet-electrode systems for critical nights
Integrate multi-modal biosensors with real-time artifact detection algorithms
Deploy quantum dot-based flexible EEG arrays for improved signal integrity
levers.review_lever: Controls signal fidelity vs. participant comfort. Weakness: The options fail to consider longitudinal sensor degradation in domestic environments.

levers.name: Participant Recruitment Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Limiting to sleep clinic referrals creates selection bias → Systemic: 30% underrepresentation of undiagnosed cases (per 2022 epidemiological data) → Strategic: Reduces generalizability of parasomnia phenotyping. Controls clinical precision vs. population diversity. Weakness: The options fail to consider socioeconomic barriers to participation.
options: Expand to primary care physician networks for early detection referrals
Launch community-based sleep health workshops with screening components
Develop a telemedicine triage system for remote eligibility assessment
levers.review_lever: Controls clinical precision vs. population diversity. Weakness: The options fail to consider socioeconomic barriers to participation.

levers.name: Data Anonymization Protocol
levers.consequences: Immediate: Full video exclusion creates 40% data loss in event-rich nights → Systemic: 25% reduction in triage tool training data (per 2023 sleep research standards) → Strategic: Limits algorithm benchmarking against raw behavioral metrics. Controls privacy protection vs. data utility. Weakness: The options fail to consider synthetic data generation as an alternative.
options: Implement differential privacy techniques for video data processing
Develop 3D motion capture as a non-identifiable alternative to video
Create a federated learning framework for distributed data analysis
levers.review_lever: Controls privacy protection vs. data utility. Weakness: The options fail to consider synthetic data generation as an alternative.

levers.name: Staffing Model Optimization
levers.consequences: Immediate: 3-person night shift rotation creates 20% burnout risk (per 2024 healthcare workforce studies) → Systemic: 15% increase in annotation errors during extended shifts → Strategic: Compromises inter-rater reliability metrics. Controls workforce sustainability vs. operational continuity. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-training for critical roles.
options: Implement 4-person shift rotation with mandatory rest periods
Develop AI-assisted annotation tools for preliminary event filtering
Create a rotating on-call pool from local sleep medicine residents
levers.review_lever: Controls workforce sustainability vs. operational continuity. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-training for critical roles.

responses.summary: The levers effectively address critical operational and methodological challenges but miss a dimension of community engagement. Add 'Establish a local patient advisory board for protocol co-design' to Lever 3 to strengthen ethical alignment and recruitment efficacy. The current options insufficiently address stakeholder co-creation in protocol development, which could mitigate participant discomfort and improve compliance.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project's core tensions revolve around balancing clinical rigor with participant comfort, data fidelity with ethical constraints, and operational scalability with resource limitations. The selected levers address critical gaps in community integration, ethical governance, and long-term data utility, ensuring the facility's viability as both a research hub and a model for longitudinal sleep studies. These levers challenge assumptions about participant retention, regulatory flexibility, and interdisciplinary collaboration, which are pivotal for sustaining a 3-year residential program.

levers.name: Community Integration Protocol
levers.consequences: Immediate: Establishes local partnerships with Bonn-based health organizations → Systemic: Increases participant recruitment rates by 15–20% through trusted referrals → Strategic: Builds public trust to mitigate privacy concerns and ensure long-term facility acceptance. Controls community engagement vs. institutional autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider cultural resistance to residential sleep research in Germany.
options: Leverage University Hospital Bonn's existing regional networks for targeted outreach to sleep disorder support groups
Host public workshops on parasomnia awareness to normalize the facility's purpose and recruit self-referring participants
Develop a local advisory board of sleep clinicians and patient advocates to co-design recruitment messaging and consent processes
levers.review_lever: Controls community engagement vs. institutional autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider cultural resistance to residential sleep research in Germany.

levers.name: Ethical Oversight Framework
levers.consequences: Immediate: Implements real-time ethical review for high-risk protocols → Systemic: Reduces adverse event reporting by 30% through preemptive risk mitigation → Strategic: Positions the facility as a benchmark for ethical residential sleep research, attracting collaborative grants. Controls regulatory compliance vs. operational agility. Weakness: The options fail to consider evolving EU data protection regulations post-Brexit.
options: Integrate a rotating ethics review panel of local physicians, patient representatives, and legal experts for weekly protocol audits
Adopt a tiered consent model with optional sub-studies for participants to opt into higher-risk data collection
Deploy AI-driven risk-assessment tools to flag protocol deviations during real-time data collection
levers.review_lever: Controls regulatory compliance vs. operational agility. Weakness: The options fail to consider evolving EU data protection regulations post-Brexit.

levers.name: Longitudinal Data Governance
levers.consequences: Immediate: Standardizes data annotation workflows across all monitoring tiers → Systemic: Reduces inter-rater disagreement by 25% through centralized protocol training → Strategic: Creates a reusable data infrastructure for future parasomnia studies, enhancing the facility's academic legacy. Controls data consistency vs. researcher autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-institutional data sharing barriers.
options: Establish a central data governance committee to oversee annotation standards and resolve scoring disputes
Implement a blockchain-based audit trail for all annotation decisions to ensure transparency
Develop a modular data schema allowing sub-studies to customize data extraction without compromising core standards
levers.review_lever: Controls data consistency vs. researcher autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-institutional data sharing barriers.

levers.name: Clinical Workflow Optimization
levers.consequences: Immediate: Reduces technician response time to event triggers by 40% → Systemic: Lowers staff burnout rates by 20% through workload balancing → Strategic: Enables 24/7 operational capacity without compromising safety, critical for capturing rare parasomnia events. Controls safety protocols vs. staff efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider seasonal variations in participant activity patterns.
options: Introduce a shift-rotation algorithm that dynamically adjusts staffing based on historical event frequency data
Deploy wearable biosensors for technicians to monitor fatigue levels and trigger automatic shift swaps
Create a tiered response system where low-risk events are logged for later analysis, reserving immediate intervention for high-risk scenarios
levers.review_lever: Controls safety protocols vs. staff efficiency. Weakness: The options fail to consider seasonal variations in participant activity patterns.

levers.name: Participant Retention Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Increases repeat participation rates by 35% through incentive redesign → Systemic: Reduces acquisition costs by 18% via word-of-mouth referrals → Strategic: Ensures sufficient event volume to meet statistical power requirements for longitudinal analysis. Controls financial incentives vs. participant burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term participant fatigue from repeated stays.
options: Offer tiered compensation packages tied to participation duration and event capture rates
Create a 'sleep ambassador' program where former participants mentor new recruits and provide feedback on facility improvements
Introduce flexible stay structures with optional 'micro-stays' of 3–5 nights for participants with busy schedules
levers.review_lever: Controls financial incentives vs. participant burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term participant fatigue from repeated stays.

responses.summary: The levers effectively address critical operational and ethical dimensions but lack a focus on cross-border collaboration. Add 'International Sleep Research Collaboration Framework' to [Community Integration Protocol] to leverage global parasomnia research networks and mitigate regional data biases.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project's core tensions revolve around balancing ecological validity with data precision, participant safety with operational efficiency, and ethical rigor with scientific ambition. The selected levers address critical gaps in environmental calibration, behavioral adaptation, annotation workflows, and dynamic consent, ensuring the facility's unique longitudinal design remains both methodologically robust and ethically defensible. These levers prioritize adaptive systems over rigid protocols, enabling the project to navigate the inherent unpredictability of parasomnia research while maintaining compliance with clinical and regulatory standards.

levers.name: Environmental Contextual Calibration
levers.consequences: Immediate: Adjust ambient lighting, temperature, and acoustics to match participants' home environments → Systemic: Increases sleep quality metrics by 15% (measured via actigraphy) but requires 20% additional staff time for environmental adjustments → Strategic: Enhances naturalistic behavior capture but risks over-optimization reducing event variability critical for phenotyping. Controls participant comfort vs. data fidelity. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term environmental adaptation effects on physiological metrics.
options: Implement a phased environmental adjustment protocol based on participant feedback and sleep quality metrics.
Integrate real-time environmental sensors to dynamically modulate lighting, temperature, and acoustics during sleep cycles.
Deploy a machine learning model to predict optimal environmental settings for each participant using historical sleep data and physiological responses.
levers.review_lever: Controls participant comfort vs. data fidelity. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term environmental adaptation effects on physiological metrics.

levers.name: Residential Behavioral Adaptation Protocol
levers.consequences: Immediate: Monitor participant acclimation through movement patterns and sleep latency → Systemic: Reduces early-stay dropout rates by 12% (measured via retention analytics) but increases initial staffing costs by 8% → Strategic: Improves longitudinal data consistency but may delay event capture during adaptation phases. Controls operational efficiency vs. data completeness. Weakness: The options fail to consider cultural or socioeconomic factors influencing behavioral adaptation.
options: Structure orientation sessions with guided sleep hygiene practices and facility familiarization.
Introduce behavioral nudges via ambient cues (e.g., soundscapes, lighting) to reinforce natural sleep rhythms.
Use immersive virtual reality simulations to pre-acclimate participants to the residential environment.
levers.review_lever: Controls operational efficiency vs. data completeness. Weakness: The options fail to consider cultural or socioeconomic factors influencing behavioral adaptation.

levers.name: Multi-Layered Annotation Workflow
levers.consequences: Immediate: Apply tiered annotation criteria based on sensor fidelity → Systemic: Reduces annotation time by 25% (measured via task analysis) but decreases inter-rater reliability by 5% → Strategic: Accelerates data processing but risks over-reliance on automated thresholds. Controls speed vs. accuracy. Weakness: The options fail to consider variability in rater expertise across sites.
options: Standardize annotation procedures with fixed checklists and dual-rater verification.
Introduce a tiered annotation system with escalating complexity based on event severity and data quality.
Implement a distributed annotation network using edge computing to enable real-time data correlation and consensus-building among raters.
levers.review_lever: Controls speed vs. accuracy. Weakness: The options fail to consider variability in rater expertise across sites.

levers.name: Informed Consent Dynamic Reassessment
levers.consequences: Immediate: Conduct bi-monthly consent reviews with participants → Systemic: Increases participant retention by 10% (measured via longitudinal tracking) but adds 15% to administrative workload → Strategic: Strengthens ethical compliance but may introduce bias through repeated consent requests. Controls autonomy vs. administrative burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider cognitive load on participants with sleep disorders.
options: Schedule annual consent renewals with detailed risk-benefit summaries.
Implement bi-monthly check-ins via structured interviews to assess ongoing understanding and comfort.
Deploy a continuous feedback loop with participants through in-person sessions and digital surveys.
levers.review_lever: Controls autonomy vs. administrative burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider cognitive load on participants with sleep disorders.

levers.name: Longitudinal Engagement Sustainment
levers.consequences: Immediate: Introduce periodic progress updates and community-building activities → Systemic: Boosts participant retention by 18% (measured via attrition rates) but diverts 10% of staff capacity from data collection → Strategic: Enhances long-term data richness but may dilute focus on core objectives. Controls engagement vs. operational focus. Weakness: The options fail to consider differential motivation across participant subgroups.
options: Provide monthly newsletters highlighting study milestones and participant contributions.
Organize social events (e.g., shared meals, guided tours) to foster community bonds.
Use immersive storytelling to showcase the impact of individual data on broader scientific understanding.
levers.review_lever: Controls engagement vs. operational focus. Weakness: The options fail to consider differential motivation across participant subgroups.

levers.name: Sensor Data Synchronization Protocol
levers.consequences: Immediate: Align all sensor timestamps via atomic clock references → Systemic: Reduces data misalignment errors by 90% (measured via cross-modal correlation) but increases hardware costs by 12% → Strategic: Ensures temporal precision for event triage but may limit scalability. Controls accuracy vs. cost. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability with legacy equipment.
options: Establish manual synchronization procedures with periodic calibration checks.
Automate synchronization using script-based time-stamping across all data streams.
Implement a quantum timekeeping system for sub-millisecond precision in multi-modal data alignment.
levers.review_lever: Controls accuracy vs. cost. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability with legacy equipment.

levers.name: Residential Safety Compliance Auditing
levers.consequences: Immediate: Conduct real-time safety monitoring via IoT-enabled sensors → Systemic: Reduces incident rates by 30% (measured via incident logs) but increases data storage demands by 18% → Strategic: Enhances participant safety but may introduce surveillance concerns. Controls security vs. privacy. Weakness: The options fail to consider participant perceptions of constant monitoring.
options: Perform quarterly safety audits with documented corrective actions.
Deploy IoT-based safety monitoring systems with automated alert thresholds.
Use predictive analytics to forecast safety risks based on environmental and behavioral patterns.
levers.review_lever: Controls security vs. privacy. Weakness: The options fail to consider participant perceptions of constant monitoring.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: [a specific, domain-relevant second-order impact with a measurable indicator, such as a % change, capacity shift, or cost delta]"
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     • State the trade-off explicitly: "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]."
     • Identify a specific weakness: "Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:", "Conservative:", "Moderate:", "Radical:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.

**Explanation:** This plan *unequivocally requires* a physical location (the research unit in Bonn, Germany), physical renovations, physical equipment, in-person staffing, and physical participants. The entire research project *revolves around* physical data collection and analysis in a real-world setting. The plan also requires physical travel to the location.
